sections.title: Overview
sections.content_md:
CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete** -- the four fundamental operations you can perform on any piece of stored data. If you have ever inserted a row into a pandas DataFrame, queried it, changed a value, and dropped it, you have performed CRUD operations.

In WellBegun, every entity type (Note, Project, Source, Actor, Activity, Plan, Collection, Log) supports the same four operations. The pattern repeats so consistently that once you understand CRUD for Notes, you understand it for all eight entity types.

CRUD is universal because it maps directly to HTTP methods (POST, GET, PUT, DELETE) and SQL statements (INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE).

key_takeaways: CRUD is the universal pattern for data manipulation in any application
Each operation maps to an HTTP method and a SQL statement
WellBegun implements CRUD identically for every entity type through a router -> service -> model architecture
Understanding the Notes CRUD gives you the template for every other entity

sections.title: Core Concepts
sections.content_md:
### The Four Operations

| CRUD | HTTP Method | SQL Statement | What it does |
|------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **C**reate | POST | INSERT | Add a new record |
| **R**ead | GET | SELECT | Retrieve one or many records |
| **U**pdate | PUT | UPDATE | Modify an existing record |
| **D**elete | DELETE | DELETE | Remove a record |

### How WellBegun Layers CRUD

Each CRUD operation flows through three layers:

1. **Router** -- defines HTTP endpoints, validates input, returns HTTP responses
2. **Service** -- contains business logic, interacts with the database
3. **Model** -- defines the database table structure

This separation means the router never touches the database directly, and the service never deals with HTTP status codes.

### Create = POST = INSERT

Creating a note sends JSON, validates it, creates a database row, and returns the new record.

### Read = GET = SELECT

Reading comes in two flavors: list all (`GET /`) or get one by ID (`GET /{id}`).

### Update = PUT = UPDATE

Updating accepts partial data via `model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` and applies only the fields sent.

### Delete = DELETE = DELETE

Deleting removes the record and cleans up related entity tags and knowledge graph references.

key_takeaways: CRUD flows through three layers: Router (HTTP) -> Service (logic) -> Model (database)
Read has two forms: list all and get by ID
Update uses exclude_unset=True for partial updates; Delete cleans up related data

sections.title: In This Project
sections.content_md:
WellBegun implements CRUD through three layers for each entity. The Notes entity demonstrates the complete pattern.

The Pydantic schemas define the data contract: `NoteCreate` (what goes in on create), `NoteUpdate` (what goes in on update), `NoteOut` (what comes out). The SQLAlchemy model defines the database table. The frontend mirrors the four operations with matching fetch functions.

code_examples.annotation: CREATE -- POST /api/notes/ validates input via NoteCreate and returns 201 on success.
READ -- two endpoints: GET / returns all notes, GET /{id} returns one or 404.
UPDATE -- PUT /api/notes/{id} uses exclude_unset=True for partial updates.
DELETE -- DELETE /api/notes/{id} removes the note and returns confirmation.

code_examples.code:
def create(
    db: Session,
    title: str,
    content: str | None = None,
) -> Note:
    note = Note(title=title, content=content)
    db.add(note)
    db.flush()
    create_entity_tag(db, title, "note", "note", note.id)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(note)
    return note
code_examples.annotation: Service CREATE -- creates the SQLAlchemy object, flushes to get the ID, creates an entity tag, commits, and returns.
Service UPDATE -- uses setattr to apply only the provided kwargs, keeping other fields unchanged.

code_examples.code:
def delete(db: Session, note_id: int) -> bool:
    note = get_by_id(db, note_id)
    if not note:
        return False
    delete_entity_tag(db, "note", note_id)
    delete_entity_graph_data(db, "note", note_id)
    db.delete(note)
    db.commit()
    return True
code_examples.annotation: Service DELETE -- cleans up entity tags and graph data before deleting the note to prevent orphaned references.

code_examples.code:
class NoteCreate(BaseModel):
    title: str
    content: str | None = None


class NoteUpdate(BaseModel):
    title: str | None = None
    content: str | None = None


class NoteOut(BaseModel):
    id: int
    title: str
    content: str | None = None
    is_active: bool
    is_archived: bool
    created_at: datetime
    updated_at: datetime

    model_config = {"from_attributes": True}
code_examples.annotation: Three schemas for three roles: NoteCreate requires title, NoteUpdate makes everything optional, NoteOut includes server-generated fields.
The frontend mirrors all four CRUD operations with matching fetch functions.

sections.title: Guided Examples
sections.content_md:
### Full CRUD Lifecycle for a Note

**1. Create** -- User types "Meeting Notes" and clicks save. Frontend sends `POST /api/notes/` with `{"title": "Meeting Notes", "content": "Discussed roadmap"}`. Backend returns the note with `id: 42` and status 201.

**2. Read** -- The note list page loads. Frontend sends `GET /api/notes/`. Backend queries `SELECT * FROM notes ORDER BY created_at DESC` and returns the full list.

**3. Update** -- User edits the title. Frontend sends `PUT /api/notes/42` with `{"title": "Q1 Meeting Notes"}`. Only title changes; content is preserved because `exclude_unset=True` ignores fields not sent.

**4. Delete** -- User removes the note. Frontend sends `DELETE /api/notes/42`. Backend cleans up entity tags and graph data, deletes the note, returns `{"ok": true}`.

code_examples.annotation: Complete CRUD lifecycle from the frontend perspective, using the four API functions.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.content_md:
### Exercise 1: Identify CRUD in Another Entity

**Task**: Open `src/wellbegun/routers/sources.py` and `src/wellbegun/services/source_service.py`. Identify the function names for each CRUD operation. How do they compare to the notes equivalents?

### Exercise 2: Trace the Update Path

**Task**: When the frontend sends `PUT /api/notes/42` with `{"content": "Updated text"}`, what SQL statement does SQLAlchemy generate? What value does `data.model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` return?

### Exercise 3: Add a Field

**Task**: Suppose you want to add a `priority` field (integer, optional, defaults to 0) to Notes. Which three files would you need to modify, and what would you add to each?

exercises.task: Open src/wellbegun/routers/sources.py and src/wellbegun/services/source_service.py. Identify the function names for each CRUD operation.
hints: Look for functions matching: list_* / get_* (Read), create_* (Create), update_* (Update), delete_* (Delete).
The naming convention is identical across all entity types.
exercises.solution_reference: src/wellbegun/routers/sources.py and src/wellbegun/services/source_service.py -- same pattern as notes: get_all, get_by_id, create, update, delete

exercises.task: When the frontend sends PUT /api/notes/42 with {"content": "Updated text"}, what does model_dump(exclude_unset=True) return? What SQL is generated?
hints: exclude_unset=True means only fields explicitly provided in the JSON body are included.
exercises.solution_reference: model_dump returns {"content": "Updated text"}. SQL: UPDATE notes SET content = 'Updated text', updated_at = '...' WHERE id = 42

exercises.task: Add a priority field (integer, optional, defaults to 0) to Notes. Which files need changes?
hints: Think about the three layers: model, schema, service.
The service uses **kwargs and setattr, so it handles new fields automatically.
exercises.solution_reference: 1. src/wellbegun/models/note.py -- add priority column. 2. src/wellbegun/schemas/note.py -- add to NoteCreate, NoteUpdate, NoteOut. 3. Service and router need no changes due to **kwargs pattern.

sections.title: Knowledge Check
sections.content_md: Test your understanding of CRUD operations as implemented in WellBegun.

questions.question: Which HTTP method corresponds to the 'Update' operation in CRUD?
options: POST
GET
PUT
DELETE
questions.explanation: PUT is used to update an existing resource. In WellBegun, PUT /api/notes/{id} updates a note (src/wellbegun/routers/notes.py:29).

questions.question: What does data.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) do in the update endpoint?
options: Removes all None values from the data
Returns only the fields the client explicitly included in the request
Converts the Pydantic model to a SQLAlchemy model
Validates that all required fields are present
questions.explanation: exclude_unset=True returns only the fields the client explicitly sent. If the client sends {"title": "New"}, content is not included and therefore not overwritten.

questions.question: Why does the delete service function clean up entity tags and graph data before deleting the note?
options: SQLAlchemy requires it for all delete operations
To prevent orphaned references in related tables
To make the delete operation slower and safer
The tags and graph data are stored in the same table
questions.explanation: The entity_tags and knowledge_triples tables reference notes by type and ID. Deleting without cleanup would leave dangling references (src/wellbegun/services/note_service.py:44-52).

questions.question: Which SQL statement is equivalent to db.query(Note).filter(Note.id == 5).first()?
options: INSERT INTO notes VALUES (5)
SELECT * FROM notes WHERE id = 5 LIMIT 1
UPDATE notes SET id = 5
DELETE FROM notes WHERE id = 5
questions.explanation: .filter(Note.id == 5) adds the WHERE clause, and .first() limits to one result (returning None if no match).

questions.question: Why are there separate NoteCreate and NoteUpdate schemas?
options: FastAPI requires separate schemas for each HTTP method
Create requires title (mandatory), while Update makes all fields optional for partial updates
One is for the frontend and the other is for the backend
They have different security permissions
questions.explanation: NoteCreate has title: str (required) because every note needs a name. NoteUpdate has title: str | None = None because you might only want to change the content.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.content_md: Curated resources for deepening your understanding of CRUD operations.
